Just now

If you’ve started learning web development, you’ve probably seen the term HTTP Request.
It sounds technical, but the idea behind it is actually very simple.
Every time you open a website, search on Google, or watch a YouTube video, HTTP requests are working behind the scenes.
Let’s understand it with an example.
Imagine Sending a Letter
Suppose you want to ask your friend a question.
You write a letter and send it.
Your friend reads it, writes a reply, and sends it back.
This is exactly how an HTTP request works.

• You send a request.

• The server receives it.

• The server processes it.

• The server sends a response.
Communication complete.
A Real Example
Let’s say you open your favorite weather app.
You tap Refresh.
What happens?
The app sends an HTTP request to the weather server asking:
“What is today’s weather?”
The server checks its database and sends back information like:

• Temperature

• Humidity

• Wind speed
Your app simply displays the result.
Every Website Uses HTTP Requests
Think about the websites you use every day.
When you:

• Log in to Instagram

• Search on Google

• Watch a YouTube video

• Order food online
Your device is constantly sending HTTP requests and receiving HTTP responses.
Without them, websites wouldn’t work.
What Does a Request Contain?
A request usually includes:

• The address of the website (URL)

• The action you want to perform

• Sometimes extra information, such as login details or search text
The server uses this information to understand what you need.
What Is an HTTP Response?
A response is simply the server’s reply.
It may contain:

• A web page

• A JSON response

• An image

• A video

• An error message
Whatever you asked for, the server tries to send back.
Why Developers Should Learn This
When you start building websites or mobile apps, you’ll use HTTP requests almost every day.
Whether you’re calling an API, logging in, uploading files, or displaying data, HTTP requests are involved.
Understanding this concept makes many other topics much easier to learn.
Final Thoughts
An HTTP request is simply a message sent from a client to a server asking for something.
The server processes that request and sends a response.
That’s it.
Once you understand this simple communication, concepts like APIs, REST APIs, and backend development become much easier to understand.
